scope: Source-backed fusion and temporal-modeling mechanics for multimodal perception systems, with emphasis on when to use gated fusion, latent bottlenecks, deformable cross-attention, and selective state-space memory.
source_policy: Primary sources only: official papers, official repositories, and official project/model pages.
design_laws: Use cross-attention when the problem is alignment to external evidence; use state-space memory when the problem is long-horizon temporal carry.
Gating belongs on fusion paths that need trust calibration; it is not a replacement for a temporal memory model.
Latent bottlenecks are useful only if they preserve the world information the downstream geometry bridge actually needs.

entries.title: Gated Cross-Attention
entries.core_trick: Use a multiplicative or residual gate on cross-attention updates so the model can decide whether and how strongly to fuse external information into the current token stream.
entries.intuition: Gated cross-attention is a lookup-and-trust mechanism. It helps when the model must decide whether to read from another modality or memory source right now instead of blindly fusing everything.
ablation_takeaways: The gain comes from selective fusion, not just adding another attention block.
Gating helps most when external information is valuable but uneven in reliability or relevance.
ablation_nuggets: Use it for camera-to-BEV or proposal-to-region fusion where external evidence is informative but noisy.
Do not confuse gated fusion with temporal memory; it solves a different problem.
apply_when: you need explicit cross-modal alignment between query tokens and external visual or geometric evidence
the system benefits from learning when to trust camera, LiDAR, region-prior, or teacher cues
avoid_when: the main bottleneck is long-horizon temporal carry rather than source selection
you already know one modality should always dominate and the gate only adds unnecessary degrees of freedom
entries.integration_pattern: Use gated cross-attention in spatial fusion blocks, especially camera-to-world or proposal-to-region fusion, then hand the fused state to a separate temporal memory mechanism.
failure_modes: using gating as a substitute for missing geometric supervision
letting the gate saturate so one modality collapses or the fusion path effectively turns off
entries.teacher_role: teacher-to-student fusion target or region-prior fusion template
entries.student_role: student fusion block for selective external evidence use
tsqbev_actions: use gated cross-attention for camera feature fusion and teacher-region prior injection
treat it as a spatial fusion mechanism, then pair it with a separate temporal memory design

entries.title: Deformable Cross-Attention
entries.core_trick: Restrict cross-attention to a small set of learned sampling locations instead of attending densely to every token or feature cell.
entries.intuition: If a query only needs a few spatially meaningful neighborhoods, learned sparse sampling is better than full dense attention. It preserves alignment while cutting compute and noise.
ablation_takeaways: Sparse learned sampling beats naive dense attention when the target structure is localized.
The benefit is largest when multiscale features matter and the model can learn where to sample.
ablation_nuggets: Use this for BEV or proposal queries that only need a few multi-view or multiscale glimpses.
Pair with perspective supervision so the learned sampling stays geometrically meaningful.
apply_when: cross-view or camera-to-world attention is too dense and expensive
queries should focus on a few spatial support regions rather than the full feature map
avoid_when: you have no reliable spatial prior and learned sparse sampling collapses arbitrarily
the task needs uniform global aggregation more than localized evidence
entries.integration_pattern: Use as the sparse spatial retrieval layer inside camera-to-BEV or query-to-image fusion, then send the fused state into the temporal model.
failure_modes: sampling points drift without geometric supervision
too few samples hurt small or thin structures that need more support
tsqbev_actions: prefer deformable cross-attention over dense cross-attention for multi-view sparse fusion
combine with perspective supervision instead of relying on sampling alone

entries.title: Q-Former Latent Query Bottleneck
entries.core_trick: Use a small learned query set to extract only the most relevant information from a large frozen foundation backbone, creating a compact downstream interface.
entries.intuition: Latent queries are a compression interface, not just a smaller transformer. They are useful when the foundation encoder is strong but too expensive or too verbose to expose directly to the downstream model.
ablation_takeaways: A latent bottleneck helps when the foundation encoder is strong and the downstream module only needs a compact task-specific summary.
Too aggressive a bottleneck destroys precisely the dense spatial cues needed for geometry-heavy tasks.
ablation_nuggets: Use latent queries to compress DINOv3 features before expensive fusion, not to erase geometry.
Retain multi-view structure or attach view-aware queries if the downstream task is 3D.
apply_when: foundation camera features are strong but too expensive to pass through directly
you need a compact queryable interface between a frozen backbone and a geometry-aware downstream head
avoid_when: the downstream task needs dense correspondence that a tiny latent bottleneck cannot preserve
you have not yet proven that the foundation features are useful at all
entries.integration_pattern: Insert a view-aware latent query bottleneck between the DINOv3 backbone and the sparse/BEV fusion path, then supervise the bridge with perspective and teacher BEV targets.
failure_modes: compressing away the fine-grained cues required for 3D localization
using a generic latent summary that mixes views and destroys calibration structure
tsqbev_actions: test view-aware latent queries as a compression layer between DINOv3 and the sparse student
do not deploy a latent bottleneck without explicit geometry and perspective supervision

entries.title: Selective SSM Temporal Memory
entries.core_trick: Carry forward a learned internal state over time with selective input-dependent updates instead of repeatedly attending over the full history.
entries.intuition: Selective SSMs solve the temporal carry problem. They are about what state survives and evolves over time, not about which external source to read from at a given moment.
ablation_takeaways: The win comes from linear-time sequence handling and persistent state, not from acting like another cross-attention module.
SSMs work best when they are asked to remember and evolve state, not to replace spatial alignment machinery wholesale.
ablation_nuggets: Use SSMs to replace temporal attention first, not spatial fusion attention.
Keep spatial fusion explicit and let the SSM carry the fused world state forward.
apply_when: temporal sequence length or streaming latency dominates the model cost
you already have a decent per-frame or per-step fusion state and need a better way to carry it over time
avoid_when: the real bottleneck is spatial or cross-modal alignment, not temporal carry
you are trying to use an SSM to compensate for missing world grounding or fusion supervision
entries.integration_pattern: Use gated or deformable cross-attention for spatial fusion, then swap temporal self-attention or queue processing for a selective SSM block.
failure_modes: replacing the wrong attention block and losing spatial precision
state drift over long horizons if the fused inputs are poor or uncalibrated
entries.student_role: high-ROI temporal module once spatial fusion is stable
tsqbev_actions: replace temporal attention after the spatial fusion path is fixed, not before
keep a clean separation: cross-attention for fusion, SSM for temporal carry
